title: Backend Healthcheck & Error Envelope Pattern
summary: Standardized healthcheck endpoint pattern (GET /health returning JSON with status, version, uptime, and dependency checks) combined with a structured error envelope (code, message, request_id, timestamp) for all API error responses. These are small, self-contained modules with clear boundaries that can be directly adapted into any backend service.
why_reusable: Every backend service needs healthcheck and error envelopes. The patterns are small (~30 lines each), have no unusual dependencies, are framework-agnostic (works with FastAPI, Flask, Express, etc.), and follow standard conventions that operations teams and monitoring tools expect.
adapter_target: Any backend service (FastAPI, Express, Flask, etc.) — copy the pattern, not the code, and adapt to the target framework's response format.
implementation_notes: Healthcheck: return JSON with status (ok/degraded/down), version, uptime_seconds, and optional checks dict for DB/redis/queue connectivity
Error envelope: always include error.code (machine-readable), error.message (human-readable), error.request_id (for log correlation), and error.timestamp
Error codes should follow a taxonomy: VALIDATION_ERROR, NOT_FOUND, UNAUTHORIZED, INTERNAL_ERROR, etc.
Never expose stack traces or internal details in error responses — log them server-side, return sanitized envelope to client
Healthcheck should be fast (<100ms) and not trigger heavy dependency checks on every call; use a cached status with background refresh for expensive checks
tests_required: Unit test: healthcheck returns 200 with expected JSON shape
Unit test: healthcheck returns 503 when a critical dependency is down
Unit test: error envelope builder includes all required fields
Unit test: error envelope sanitizes stack traces
Integration test: healthcheck reflects real DB/redis status
risks: Healthcheck must not leak version info that aids attackers (consider a /health/auth variant)
Error envelope must be consistent across all endpoints — enforce via middleware, not per-endpoint
Request ID propagation requires middleware at the entry point; missing it means lost correlation
Healthcheck dependency checks can DDOS your own database if called frequently by orchestration
do_not_do: Do not expose stack traces in error responses
Do not make healthcheck endpoints do heavy work
Do not use different error shapes for different endpoints
Do not skip request ID generation — untraceable errors are undebuggable
dependencies: none (stdlib only for basic healthcheck; FastAPI/Express for framework integration)
packaging_impact: None — pure application code, no additional packages
notes: Derived from backend domain curated rules on error handling and logging. This is a universally applicable pattern — the asset describes the interface shape and conventions, not copied source code.
